title: A Grand Strategy of Consolidation: How Trump Can Revitalize American Power
content:
The country’s new defense strategy envisioned a dramatic shakeup that prioritized the homeland and repositioned forces that had patrolled distant frontiers for nearly a century. It handed the task of securing farther-flung defensive perimeters to allies, many of which appeared unprepared to take on the burden. Establishment experts were appalled, and hawks warned that the new strategy would embolden adversaries. They advocated for the old approach of being strong everywhere at once. The year was 1904, and the country was the United Kingdom, facing a dilemma broadly similar to the one the United States now confronts.

Its empire was the world’s strongest power, but its strategic situation was deteriorating rapidly as rising powers surpassed it in industrial production. Imperial Germany was building a formidable fleet, while France and Russia mounted fresh challenges. British leaders had a choice: they could keep trying to outgun all competitors or try something new. The country’s top admiral, John Fisher, opted for the latter by outlining a strategy of consolidation. Consolidation occurs when a state focuses on its foremost interests while ramping up national resources to increase its power over time.

Instead of trying to maintain all of the British Empire’s far-flung naval stations, Fisher prioritized the waters adjacent to the British Isles to deter Germany. To cover the gaps created elsewhere, he aimed to rely on regional allies that British diplomats were actively courting. This allowed the United Kingdom to buy time, mobilize its powerful industries, and achieve a concentrated defense. The United States is at a similar juncture today in its global posture. Facing new great-power rivalries, American strategists may need to study the British example of strategic consolidation.
